text: This is a robotic Roux-en-Y repair of an E-IV bile duct injury following a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
With over seven hundred and fifty thousand laparoscopic cholecystectomies performed in the US, bile duct injuries continue to be a real problem that requires real solutions.
The surgeon clears the lower third of the gallbladder, exposing the doublet view, also known as the critical view of safety, which reveals key anatomical landmarks for safe dissection.
Our case concerns a sixty-eight-year-old female with choledocholithiasis who developed gallstone pancreatitis.
She had an ERCP with sphincterotomy and then came back for an interval lap chole.
The surgeon encounters adhesions between the alimentum and duodenum, with visible edema and inflammation. A stapler is fired across the infundibulum of the gallbladder.
The intraoperative cholangiogram is displayed on the left side of the screen, providing a visual assessment of the bile ducts during the procedure.
Post-operative HIDA study showed a significant leak from the gallbladder fossa and post-op MRCP actually showed complete loss of the duct approximately at the bifurcation.
A cholangiogram also shot post-operatively
Our concern based on preoperative studies was an E three injury at the confluence of the left and right bile ducts.
Upon entering the abdomen, a large biloma is visible. After cleaning the area of blood and bile, the surgeon identifies the bile duct injuries.

The surgeon cannulates the left biliary system using a percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTHC) catheter placed by interventional radiology, aiding in the identification of the bile duct anatomy.
The surgeon creates a jejunal-jejunal anastomosis using a linear stapler and a spiral knotless absorbable suture, visibly connecting the two sections of the jejunum.
The surgeon closes the defect in the mesentery using a running suture technique, ensuring the mesenteric tissue is securely approximated.
The surgeon ties the left biliary system with an absorbable suture, followed by cannulation using the PTHC catheter.
The surgeon advances the pigtail catheter into the hepatic vaginostomy within the left biliary system.
The surgeon completes the anterior row of sutures in a running fashion using a 5-0 absorbable suture.
The surgeon sutures the right biliary system closed, completing the repair with absorbable sutures.
The surgeon completes the anterior layer of the hepaticojejunostomy on the anterior right bile duct using a running suture technique.
The surgeon closes all visible mesenteric defects using sutures.
The surgeon divides the falciform ligament and positions it to cover the newly created anastomoses. A surgical drain is then placed in the area.
An anterior cholangiogram reveals a free leak in the right posterior biliary system, leading to the placement of an internal-external catheter.
She was seen back in clinic and her intraperitoneal drains were removed.
Two months out from surgery, she had another IR cholangiogram, which showed that all three bile ducts were completely patent when their catheters were backed up.
It's worth noting that there are some larger retrospective studies on robotic-assisted HJs now.

In summary, the robotic hepatic ojejunostomy in this patient was not only a safe approach, but may have actually benefited her in discovering and repairing efficiently an injury that was not noted in the preoperative workup.
